title: ARP Spoofing / ARP Poisoning
description: Sending fake ARP replies to poison ARP caches and intercept network traffic.
fullExplanation: ARP has no authentication — any device can send an ARP reply claiming any IP-to-MAC mapping. An attacker sends gratuitous ARP replies to both the victim and the gateway: telling the victim 'the gateway MAC is MY MAC' and telling the gateway 'the victim MAC is MY MAC'. Both update their ARP caches. All traffic between victim and gateway now flows through the attacker — a classic Man-in-the-Middle position.

attackSteps.action: Attacker sends ARP Reply to victim
attackSteps.effect: Victim's ARP cache poisoned — gateway IP maps to attacker MAC

attackSteps.action: Attacker sends ARP Reply to gateway
attackSteps.effect: Gateway's ARP cache poisoned — victim IP maps to attacker MAC
Traffic forwarded so victim doesn't notice

attackSteps.action: All victim traffic flows through attacker
attackSteps.payload: Attacker can read, modify, or drop packets
attackSteps.effect: Full MITM — credentials, session tokens, data exposed

keyFacts: ARP has no authentication — completely trust-based
Attack works on any switched network
Attacker must be on the same subnet
Can be used to intercept HTTP, steal cookies, downgrade HTTPS
Continuous ARP replies needed — caches expire
Detectable by monitoring ARP traffic for duplicates

defenses.name: Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI)
defenses.description: Switch validates ARP packets against DHCP snooping binding table. Drops invalid ARP replies.

defenses.name: DHCP Snooping
defenses.description: Builds trusted IP-MAC-port binding table used by DAI.

defenses.name: Static ARP Entries
defenses.description: Manually configure IP-MAC mappings for critical hosts. Not scalable.

defenses.name: 802.1X Port Authentication
defenses.description: Only authenticated devices can connect to switch ports.

defenses.name: XArp / ARPwatch
defenses.description: Monitor ARP traffic and alert on suspicious changes.

defenses.name: Encrypted Protocols
defenses.description: HTTPS, SSH, TLS protect data even if MITM succeeds.
